title: Asclepias scabra
taxonomy: Asclepias scabra is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. It is native to North America and is part of the Asclepias genus, which includes milkweed plants. The Asclepias scabra is also commonly known as sandhill milkweed. This classification places it within the order Gentianales and the clade Asterids. As a member of the Apocynaceae family, Asclepias scabra shares characteristics with other plants that produce milky sap.
uses: Asclepias scabra, also known as the sandhill milkweed, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots and other parts are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. In some cultures, it has been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other conditions. However, the effectiveness and safety of using Asclepias scabra as a medicinal plant require further research and validation. Traditional use of this plant should be approached with caution and under the guidance of a qualified healthcare professional.

constituents: Asclepias scabra contains a variety of active constituents, including alkaloids and glycosides. These compounds, particularly the cardenolides, have been found to have medicinal properties. preparations: Asclepias scabra is not commonly referenced in available literature, so I'll provide general information on herbal preparations for Asclepias genus. side_effects: Asclepias scabra, commonly known as the hairy milkweed, is not typically associated with significant side effects in humans. However, like other plants in the Asclepias genus, it does contain cardenolides, which can be toxic if ingested in large quantities. Ingestion of the plant can cause vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain in humans and pets. The milky sap of the plant can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and avoid ingesting any parts of it.
intro: Asclepias scabra is a perennial plant species within the Apocynaceae family, commonly known as a milkweed plant. Traditionally, Asclepias scabra has been used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and as a diuretic, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Asclepias scabra include cardenolides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties, as well as alkaloids and glycosides. The most common herbal preparations made with Asclepias scabra include teas, infusions, and tinctures, although its use in herbal medicine is not widely established and may require further research and caution.
